planting.note: Direct sow in fall for natural cold stratification and spring germination; in mild climates can also be sown in early spring. Spreads vigorously by stolons and self-seeds freely once established.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich soil in partial shade to light morning sun; in zone 8–9 this European violet stays semi-evergreen in winter and begins flowering as early as December in mild years, providing critical early nectar for emerging queen bumblebees. The fragrant violet or white flowers are fully edible and prized for crystallizing, flavoring syrups, and garnishing desserts and cocktails; the leaves are also edible raw or cooked as greens. The plant spreads energetically by stolons and self-seeds, naturalizing quickly under deciduous trees and shrubs. Remove unwanted seedlings annually if spread needs to be managed in a formal garden.

planting.note: Root stem cuttings in spring or fall; this native stonecrop takes easily from even small stem sections laid on moist soil.

directions: Plant cuttings or plugs in rocky, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; this is one of the very few native sedums that thrives in shade, growing naturally on shaded stream banks and moist cliff faces where other plants struggle. The flat, succulent rosettes of three-part leaves form a dense groundcover, and the starry white flowers in spring attract native bees. Unlike most sedums, it prefers consistent moisture and will not thrive in hot, dry, sunny locations. Propagate by division or stem cuttings any time during the growing season; pieces root readily when placed in contact with moist soil.

planting.note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring near water or in low-lying areas with reliable moisture; this shrub is one of the best choices for wet, poorly-drained sites where other shrubs fail.

directions: Plant in wet to regularly flooded, acidic soil in full sun to partial shade; this native shrub naturally grows at pond and stream margins across most of North America and tolerates prolonged standing water that would kill most woody plants. The spherical, fragrant creamy-white flower heads in midsummer are extraordinary magnets for butterflies, hummingbirds, and native bees — one of the highest-rated native shrubs for wildlife value in the Southeast. The unusual ball-like seed heads persist through winter, providing food for waterfowl. Allow to grow naturally into its multi-stemmed, layered form rather than shearing; it requires little pruning beyond occasional removal of dead wood.

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost or start indoors 4–6 weeks earlier for an earlier harvest; in frost-free zones sow any time of year.

directions: Sow seeds 1/4 inch deep in fertile, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade after soil warms to 70°F; the deep burgundy-red, deeply lobed foliage is ornamental throughout the season, making this one of the most attractive edible plants for a zone 9–11 garden. The young leaves have a tart, cranberry-sorrel flavor excellent in salads, wraps, and as a garnish; the fuchsia flowers are also edible and make a vivid cranberry-colored tea. In zone 9–11 it grows as a perennial but blooms only in short days, producing flowers in late fall; in cooler zones treat as a warm-season annual for leaf harvest. Cut back by half in late summer to promote fresh tender growth.

planting.note: Direct sow on soil surface (light aids germination) after last frost, or start indoors 6 weeks earlier; in zone 9–11 it self-sows freely and behaves as a naturalized perennial.

directions: Scatter seed lightly on the soil surface after last frost in well-drained, average to poor soil in full sun; this Gulf Coast native thrives in heat and drought where cultivated salvias fail, and it self-sows so freely in zone 9 that it naturalizes into a permanent colony. The brilliant red flower spikes bloom from spring through first frost and are irresistible to ruby-throated hummingbirds; white-flowering varieties attract additional butterfly species. Deadhead lightly to prolong bloom, but allow some seed to set for naturalization. It tolerates poor, sandy soil and partial shade better than many salvias, making it ideal for difficult spots in a Gulf Coast landscape.
